title: Continuous subarray sum multiple k
description: Check if subarray sum is multiple of k

examples.explanation: Start with array [23, 2, 4, 6, 7], k=6. Check all subarrays: Start i=0: j=0: sum=23, 23%6=5 ≠0; j=1: sum=25, 25%6=1 ≠0; j=2: sum=29, 29%6=5 ≠0; j=3: sum=35, 35%6=5 ≠0; j=4: sum=42, 42%6=0 → found! Return true.

Array [1, 2, 3], k=5. Check subarrays: i=0: j=0: 1%5=1; j=1: 3%5=3; j=2: 6%5=1. i=1: j=1: 2%5=2; j=2: 5%5=0 → wait, sum=5, divisible by 5! Actually 2+3=5, 5%5=0, so should return true. Correction: output should be true. Let me recalc: i=1, j=2 gives subarray [2,3] sum=5, 5%5=0 → true.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check all possible subarrays by iterating through each start and end index, compute their sums, and verify if any sum is divisible by k. Use this for small inputs or as a baseline to understand the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Solution for Continuous Subarray Sum Multiple of k
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking all possible subarrays for sum divisible by k
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the problem. The intuition is straightforward: we need to examine every possible contiguous subarray in the input array, compute its sum, and check if that sum is divisible by k. Since we're checking all possibilities, we're guaranteed to find a solution if one exists. The algorithm works by using two nested loops: the outer loop fixes the starting index of the subarray, and the inner loop extends the subarray by adding elements one by one. For each subarray defined by start and end indices, we compute the sum by either accumulating as we extend or by using a prefix sum approach. We then check if the sum modulo k equals 0 (with special handling for k=0). The time complexity is O(n²) because we have n choices for start index and up to n choices for end index, resulting in roughly n²/2 subarrays to check. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach serves as a baseline for understanding the problem and is practical only for small inputs (n ≤ 1000). Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle k=0 case (where we check if sum equals 0 rather than using modulo), not considering subarrays of length 1, and inefficiently recomputing sums from scratch instead of accumulating. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete algorithm using the key subroutine of checking individual subarrays.

examples.trace: Start with array [23, 2, 4, 6, 7], k=6. Check all subarrays: Start i=0: j=0: sum=23, 23%6=5 ≠0; j=1: sum=25, 25%6=1 ≠0; j=2: sum=29, 29%6=5 ≠0; j=3: sum=35, 35%6=5 ≠0; j=4: sum=42, 42%6=0 → found! Return true.

Array [1, 2, 3], k=5. Check subarrays: i=0: j=0: 1%5=1; j=1: 3%5=3; j=2: 6%5=1. i=1: j=1: 2%5=2; j=2: 5%5=0 → wait, sum=5, divisible by 5! Actually 2+3=5, 5%5=0, so should return true. Correction: output should be true. Let me recalc: i=1, j=2 gives subarray [2,3] sum=5, 5%5=0 → true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking All Subarrays Starting from a Fixed Index
ladders.desc: For a given start index, check all subarrays extending to the right
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach. Given a fixed starting index i in the array, we need to check all possible subarrays that begin at i and end at any index j where i ≤ j < n. The intuition is that by fixing the start, we can efficiently extend the subarray one element at a time, accumulating the sum as we go. This avoids recomputing sums from scratch for each subarray. The algorithm: Initialize current_sum = 0. For j from i to n-1: add nums[j] to current_sum, then check if current_sum is divisible by k (with special handling for k=0 where we check if sum == 0). If found, return true. If we finish all j for this i without success, return false. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n) in worst case (when i=0 and we check all j). Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 as the inner loop of the complete solution. Common mistakes: not resetting current_sum for each new i, incorrect modulo operation with negative numbers (use (sum % k + k) % k in some languages), and off-by-one errors in loop bounds. The real pattern: for i in range(n): sum=0; for j in range(i, n): sum+=nums[j]; if k==0: if sum==0: return True; else: if sum%k==0: return True.

examples.trace: Start i=0, sum=0. j=0: sum=2, 2%6=2 ≠0; j=1: sum=6, 6%6=0 → found! Return true.
Start i=1, sum=0. j=1: sum=3, 3%4=3 ≠0; j=2: sum=8, 8%4=0 → actually 3+5=8, 8%4=0, so should return true. Correction: output true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking if Sum of Subarray is Divisible by k
ladders.desc: Given a subarray defined by start and end indices, determine if its sum is a multiple of k
ladders.explanation: This is the central logical check of the problem. Given an array, start index i, end index j, and integer k, we need to determine if the sum of elements from i to j (inclusive) is divisible by k. The intuition uses modular arithmetic: a number is divisible by k if its remainder when divided by k is 0. So we compute sum = nums[i] + nums[i+1] + ... + nums[j], then check if sum % k == 0. Special case: when k=0, division by zero is undefined, so we check if sum == 0 instead (since only 0 is divisible by 0). The algorithm: First compute the sum (either by iterating from i to j or using precomputed prefix sums). Then if k == 0: return (sum == 0); else: return (sum % k == 0). Time complexity is O(j-i+1) to compute sum if not precomputed. Space is O(1). This feeds into L1 as the condition check after accumulating the sum. Common mistakes: forgetting k=0 case, integer overflow for large sums (use modulo during accumulation), and incorrect handling of negative numbers in modulo (in Python % always returns non-negative, but in some languages negative remainders need adjustment). The real pattern: sum=0; for idx in range(i, j+1): sum+=nums[idx]; if k==0: return sum==0; else: return sum%k==0.

examples.trace: Subarray [4,5,6], sum=15, 15%3=0 → true.
Subarray [2,3], sum=5, 5%4=1 ≠0 → false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Sum of a Contiguous Subarray
ladders.desc: Calculate the sum of elements between two indices in an array
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental to the problem: given an array and two indices i and j (with i ≤ j), compute the sum of all elements from index i to j inclusive. The intuition is simple accumulation: we iterate through the specified range and add each element to a running total. This is a basic array operation that appears in many algorithms. The algorithm: Initialize sum = 0. For index from i to j (inclusive): sum = sum + nums[index]. Return sum. Time complexity is O(j-i+1), which is O(n) in worst case. Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 as the first step before checking divisibility. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (including j or not), empty subarray when i>j (but problem guarantees i≤j), and integer overflow for large numbers (though less common in Python). Optimization note: for repeated queries, we can use prefix sums to answer in O(1) time after O(n) preprocessing, but that's not needed for brute force. The real pattern: sum=0; for idx in range(i, j+1): sum+=nums[idx]; return sum.

examples.trace: Indices 1 to 3: nums[1]=2, nums[2]=3, nums[3]=4. Sum=2+3+4=9.
Indices 0 to 2: nums[0]=5, nums[1]=-2, nums[2]=7. Sum=5+(-2)+7=10.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Array Indices with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested loops to generate all possible start and end index pairs
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is about generating all possible (i, j) pairs where i ≤ j, representing all contiguous subarrays of an array. The intuition: to examine every subarray, we need to consider every possible starting point i, and for each i, every possible ending point j that comes after i. This is achieved with two nested loops: outer loop for i from 0 to n-1, inner loop for j from i to n-1. This generates exactly n(n+1)/2 pairs, covering all subarrays. The algorithm: n = length of array. For i in range(0, n): for j in range(i, n): process subarray from i to j. Time complexity is O(n²). Space is O(1). This feeds into L3 as the mechanism to select which subarray to sum. Common mistakes: starting j from 0 instead of i (which would process non-contiguous elements or duplicate), incorrect loop bounds causing index errors, and not considering empty subarrays (which aren't needed here). This pattern is foundational for many brute force algorithms on arrays. The real pattern: n=len(nums); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): # now have subarray nums[i..j].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices and iterate with loops
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how arrays (or lists in Python) work. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index starting from 0. The intuition: think of an array like a row of boxes numbered 0, 1, 2, etc., where each box holds a value. To work with arrays, we need to know how to access individual elements (using array[index]), how to iterate through all elements (using a loop), and how to use indices to define ranges. The algorithm basics: declare an array, use for loops to traverse. For example, to print all elements: for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i]). Time complexity for traversing is O(n). Space is O(1) for the traversal itself. This feeds into L4 as the basic skill needed to implement nested loops. Common beginner mistakes: using index 1 as first element (should be 0), going out of bounds (index >= length), and confusing the element value with its index. This foundation is essential for any array manipulation problem. The real pattern: arr = [1, 2, 3]; for i in range(len(arr)): print(f'Index {i}: value {arr[i]}').

examples.trace: Array has length 3. Loop i=0: arr[0]=10; i=1: arr[1]=20; i=2: arr[2]=30.

approaches.name: Prefix Sum with HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Compute prefix sums modulo k while storing remainders in a hash map; if the same remainder appears again or a remainder of 0 is found with at least two elements, a valid subarray exists. This leverages the property that equal remainders indicate a subarray sum divisible by k.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Subarray Sum is Multiple of K
ladders.desc: Complete solution using prefix sum with hashmap to find if any subarray sum is divisible by k
ladders.explanation: This problem requires checking if there exists a contiguous subarray whose sum is a multiple of k. The naive approach would check all O(n²) subarrays, but we can optimize to O(n) using prefix sums and modular arithmetic. The key insight: if two prefix sums have the same remainder when divided by k, then the subarray between them has a sum divisible by k. For example, if prefix_sum[i] % k = r and prefix_sum[j] % k = r (with j > i), then (prefix_sum[j] - prefix_sum[i]) % k = 0. We also handle the special case where a prefix sum itself is divisible by k (remainder 0), but we need at least two elements to form a valid subarray. We maintain a hashmap that stores the first occurrence index of each remainder. As we iterate through the array, we compute the running prefix sum modulo k. If we encounter a remainder that already exists in the hashmap and the distance between current index and stored index is at least 2, we return true. We also check if current remainder is 0 and we have processed at least 2 elements. The algorithm runs in O(n) time and O(min(n,k)) space since we store at most k remainders. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle negative numbers properly (use ((sum % k) + k) % k in languages where % can be negative), not checking the distance condition for remainder 0 case, and off-by-one errors in index tracking. This approach elegantly transforms a sum divisibility problem into a remainder tracking problem.

examples.trace:
Initialize: sum = 0, remainder_map = {0: -1}
Step 1: i=0, num=23, sum = (0+23)=23, remainder = 23%6=5, map={0:-1,5:0}, no match
Step 2: i=1, num=2, sum = (23+2)=25, remainder = 25%6=1, map={0:-1,5:0,1:1}, no match
Step 3: i=2, num=4, sum = (25+4)=29, remainder = 29%6=5, remainder 5 exists at index 0, current i=2, distance=2-0=2 >=2 → return true

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Track Remainder Occurrences with HashMap
ladders.desc: Implement the hashmap tracking logic that detects repeated remainders
ladders.explanation: The core subroutine in the full solution is maintaining a hashmap that tracks the first occurrence index of each remainder when computing prefix sums modulo k. This subroutine takes the current prefix sum remainder and current index as input, and returns whether we've found a valid subarray. The algorithm: 1. Initialize hashmap with {0: -1} to handle the case where prefix sum itself is divisible by k. 2. For each element, compute current prefix sum and its remainder modulo k. 3. Check if this remainder exists in the hashmap. If it does, and the distance between current index and stored index is at least 2, return true (found valid subarray). 4. If remainder doesn't exist, store it with current index. 5. Continue until end of array. The intuition: when two prefix sums have the same remainder, their difference (which represents a subarray sum) is divisible by k. The distance check ensures we have at least two elements in the subarray. Time complexity is O(1) per operation (hashmap lookup/insert), and space is O(min(n,k)) for storing remainders. Common mistakes include not initializing with {0: -1}, forgetting the distance check, and incorrect handling of the first occurrence storage. This subroutine is the engine that makes the O(n) solution possible.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Equal Remainders Imply Divisible Subarray Sum
ladders.desc: Understand why same remainders in prefix sums indicate divisible subarray
ladders.explanation: This is the central mathematical insight: if two prefix sums have the same remainder when divided by k, then the subarray between them has a sum divisible by k. Let's prove this: Suppose we have prefix sums P[i] and P[j] with j > i. The subarray sum from i+1 to j is P[j] - P[i]. If P[i] ≡ r (mod k) and P[j] ≡ r (mod k), then P[j] - P[i] ≡ r - r ≡ 0 (mod k). This means (P[j] - P[i]) is divisible by k. The intuition: remainder represents how much a number exceeds the nearest multiple of k. If two numbers have the same 'excess', their difference is exactly a multiple of k. We also need to handle the special case where a prefix sum itself is divisible by k (remainder 0). In this case, the subarray from start to current index has sum divisible by k, but we need at least 2 elements. This is why we initialize with a 'virtual' prefix sum of 0 at index -1. The algorithm applies this insight by tracking remainders of prefix sums as we compute them. When we see a remainder we've seen before, we know the subarray between those indices has sum divisible by k. This transforms an O(n²) subarray checking problem into an O(n) remainder tracking problem.

examples.trace:
Compute remainders: 23%6=5, 25%6=1, 29%6=5
Remainder 5 appears at indices 0 and 2
Subarray between indices 1 and 2: sum = 29-23=6, 6%6=0 ✓
Mathematical proof: 23 ≡ 5 mod 6, 29 ≡ 5 mod 6, so 29-23 ≡ 0 mod 6

Compute remainders: 5%5=0, 5%5=0, 10%5=0, 15%5=0
Remainder 0 appears multiple times
First repeated at indices 0 and 1: subarray sum = 5-5=0, 0%5=0 ✓
Also indices 0 and 2: 10-5=5, 5%5=0 ✓
The property holds for any pair with same remainder

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Prefix Sums Modulo K
ladders.desc: Calculate running prefix sums and compute their remainders modulo k
ladders.explanation: A prefix sum is the cumulative sum of elements from the start of the array up to each position. For array [a₀, a₁, ..., aₙ₋₁], prefix sum P[i] = a₀ + a₁ + ... + aᵢ. We need to compute these prefix sums efficiently as we iterate through the array. Instead of storing all prefix sums, we maintain a running sum. The key optimization: we only need the remainders of these prefix sums modulo k, not the actual sums. This is because our core logic only cares about remainders. So we compute: running_sum = (running_sum + current_element) % k. However, we must handle negative numbers carefully: in some languages, -1 % 5 = -1, but we want it to be 4. So we use: remainder = ((running_sum % k) + k) % k to ensure non-negative remainder. The algorithm: 1. Initialize running_sum = 0. 2. For each element in array: running_sum = (running_sum + element) % k, then adjust for negative if needed. 3. Store or use this remainder. This building block is essential because it gives us the remainders we need to apply the core logic. Time complexity is O(n), space is O(1) for the running sum. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle negative remainders, not updating running_sum correctly, and integer overflow (though modulo operation prevents this).

examples.trace:
Initialize sum=0
Element 23: sum = (0+23)=23, 23%6=5 → remainder 5
Element 2: sum = (23+2)=25, 25%6=1 → remainder 1
Element 4: sum = (25+4)=29, 29%6=5 → remainder 5
Final remainders: [5,1,5]

Initialize sum=0
Element -1: sum = (0+(-1))=-1, (-1%4) in Python=3, adjust: (( -1 %4)+4)%4=3 → remainder 3
Element 2: sum = (-1+2)=1, 1%4=1 → remainder 1
Element 3: sum = (1+3)=4, 4%4=0 → remainder 0
Final remainders: [3,1,0]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Arithmetic with Negative Numbers
ladders.desc: Compute a mod b correctly for both positive and negative integers
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic finds the remainder when dividing one integer by another. For positive numbers, a mod b = a % b. But for negative numbers, different programming languages handle this differently. In Python, -1 % 5 = 4 (always returns non-negative). In Java/C++, -1 % 5 = -1. For our algorithm, we need consistent non-negative remainders. The mathematical definition: a mod b should give a number r such that 0 ≤ r < b and a = qb + r for some integer q. For negative a, we need to adjust. Formula: r = ((a % b) + b) % b. This works in all languages. Example: a = -1, b = 5. Step 1: a % b = -1 (in C++) or 4 (in Python). Step 2: add b: -1+5=4 or 4+5=9. Step 3: % b: 4%5=4 or 9%5=4. Both give 4. Why do we need this? In our problem, array elements can be negative, so prefix sums can be negative. We need their remainders modulo k to be in range [0, k-1] to use in hashmap. Without this adjustment, negative remainders wouldn't match positive ones even though mathematically they represent the same congruence class. This operation is O(1) time. Common mistakes: assuming % always gives non-negative, not applying adjustment in languages where it's needed, and confusion about mathematical vs programming definitions of mod.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute a % b: In Python: -7 % 5 = 3, In C++: -7 % 5 = -2
Step 2: Apply formula ((a % b) + b) % b
Python: ((3) + 5) % 5 = 8 % 5 = 3
C++: ((-2) + 5) % 5 = 3 % 5 = 3
Both give 3, which is correct because -7 = (-2)*5 + 3

Step 1: a % b = 13 % 5 = 3 (all languages)
Step 2: ((3) + 5) % 5 = 8 % 5 = 3
Formula works for positive numbers too: 13 = 2*5 + 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prefix Sums
ladders.desc: Understand what prefix sums are and how to compute them
ladders.explanation: A prefix sum is the sum of all elements from the beginning of an array up to a certain position. For an array A of length n, the prefix sum array P is defined as: P[0] = A[0], P[1] = A[0] + A[1], P[2] = A[0] + A[1] + A[2], and so on. In general, P[i] = sum of A[0] through A[i]. We can compute prefix sums efficiently using a running sum: start with sum = 0, then for each element, add it to sum and record the value. The key property: the sum of any subarray A[i..j] can be computed as P[j] - P[i-1] (with P[-1] = 0). This is powerful because it allows us to compute any subarray sum in O(1) time after O(n) preprocessing. Example: Array [2,3,1,4]. Prefix sums: [2,5,6,10]. To get sum of subarray from index 1 to 2 (3+1=4), compute P[2] - P[0] = 6-2=4. Prefix sums transform subarray problems from O(n²) to O(n). In our main problem, we don't need the actual prefix sums array—we just need the running sum modulo k. But understanding prefix sums is fundamental to grasping why the algorithm works. This concept is beginner-friendly and essential for many array problems.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with sum=0
Index 0: add 1 → sum=1, prefix[0]=1
Index 1: add 2 → sum=3, prefix[1]=3
Index 2: add 3 → sum=6, prefix[2]=6
Index 3: add 4 → sum=10, prefix[3]=10
Index 4: add 5 → sum=15, prefix[4]=15

Step 1: sum=0
Index 0: add 3 → sum=3, prefix[0]=3
Index 1: add -1 → sum=2, prefix[1]=2
Index 2: add 2 → sum=4, prefix[2]=4
Index 3: add 0 → sum=4, prefix[3]=4

approaches.name: Prefix Sum with Array O(n)
approaches.summary: Similar to the hash map approach but uses an array of size k to store the first occurrence of each remainder, checking for repeats or zero remainders. This is efficient when k is relatively small compared to n.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Subarray Sum is Multiple of K Using Prefix Sum with Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution using prefix sums with an array of size k to store remainder first occurrences.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires checking if there exists a contiguous subarray whose sum is a multiple of k. The intuition stems from modular arithmetic: if two prefix sums have the same remainder when divided by k, then the subarray between them has a sum divisible by k. For example, if prefix_sum[i] % k = r and prefix_sum[j] % k = r (with i < j), then the sum from i+1 to j is divisible by k because (prefix_sum[j] - prefix_sum[i]) % k = 0. We use an array 'remainder_index' of size k to store the first index where each remainder occurs. Initialize remainder_index[0] = -1 to handle subarrays starting from index 0. Iterate through the array, compute running sum and remainder. If remainder is negative, adjust it to positive by adding k. Check if this remainder has been seen before (i.e., remainder_index[remainder] is not -1). If yes, and the distance between current index and stored index is at least 2 (ensuring subarray length ≥ 2 as per problem constraints), return true. Otherwise, store the current index if this remainder is seen for the first time. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array once. Space complexity is O(k) for the remainder array. This approach is efficient when k is relatively small. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of checking and updating remainder occurrences. Common mistakes include not handling negative remainders correctly, forgetting to check subarray length ≥ 2, and not initializing remainder_index[0] properly. Real code pattern: Initialize remainder_index array with -1, set remainder_index[0] = -1. For each element, update running sum, compute remainder, adjust if negative, check if remainder seen and distance ≥ 2, else store index.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize remainder_index = [-1, -1, -1, -1, -1, -1] (size 6), set remainder_index[0] = -1. Running sum = 0.
Step 2: i=0, num=23. Running sum = 23. Remainder = 23 % 6 = 5 (positive). remainder_index[5] is -1, so store index 0: remainder_index[5] = 0.
Step 3: i=1, num=2. Running sum = 25. Remainder = 25 % 6 = 1. remainder_index[1] is -1, store index 1: remainder_index[1] = 1.
Step 4: i=2, num=4. Running sum = 29. Remainder = 29 % 6 = 5. remainder_index[5] is 0 (seen before). Distance = 2 - 0 = 2 ≥ 2, so return true. Subarray from index 1 to 2: [2,4] sum=6, divisible by 6.

Step 1: Initialize remainder_index = [-1, -1, -1, -1, -1, -1], set remainder_index[0] = -1. Running sum = 0.
Step 2: i=0, num=23. Running sum = 23. Remainder = 23 % 6 = 5. remainder_index[5] = -1, store index 0: remainder_index[5] = 0.
Step 3: i=1, num=2. Running sum = 25. Remainder = 25 % 6 = 1. remainder_index[1] = -1, store index 1: remainder_index[1] = 1.
Step 4: i=2, num=6. Running sum = 31. Remainder = 31 % 6 = 1. remainder_index[1] is 1 (seen before). Distance = 2 - 1 = 1 < 2, so continue.
Step 5: i=3, num=4. Running sum = 35. Remainder = 35 % 6 = 5. remainder_index[5] is 0 (seen before). Distance = 3 - 0 = 3 ≥ 2, return true. Subarray from index 1 to 3: [2,6,4] sum=12, divisible by 6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check and Update Remainder Occurrences in Array
ladders.desc: Implement the core logic of tracking remainders using an array and detecting repeats.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the full solution. The intuition is to use an array 'remainder_index' of size k to store the first index where each remainder (from 0 to k-1) occurs when computing prefix sums modulo k. We initialize all entries to -1, except remainder_index[0] = -1 to represent the prefix sum before the array starts. As we iterate through the array, we compute the running sum and its remainder modulo k. If the remainder is negative (due to negative numbers), we adjust it to a positive value by adding k. Then, we check if this remainder has been seen before by looking at remainder_index[remainder]. If it has been seen (i.e., not -1), and the distance between the current index and the stored index is at least 2, we have found a valid subarray. Otherwise, if this remainder is seen for the first time, we store the current index in remainder_index[remainder]. This works because if two prefix sums have the same remainder, their difference is divisible by k. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(1) per element, and space is O(k). Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called in each iteration of the main loop. Common mistakes include not adjusting negative remainders, which can lead to array index errors, and not checking the distance condition properly. Real code pattern: remainder_index = [-1] * k; remainder_index[0] = -1; for each index i and num: running_sum += num; remainder = running_sum % k; if remainder < 0: remainder += k; if remainder_index[remainder] != -1 and i - remainder_index[remainder] >= 2: return True; else if remainder_index[remainder] == -1: remainder_index[remainder] = i.

examples.input: running_sum = 23, k = 6, remainder_index initially [-1, -1, -1, -1, -1, -1] with remainder_index[0] = -1, current index = 0
examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute remainder = 23 % 6 = 5 (positive).
Step 2: Check remainder_index[5] = -1 (not seen before).
Step 3: Since it's first occurrence, store index 0: remainder_index[5] = 0.
Step 4: No valid subarray found yet.

Step 1: Compute remainder = 29 % 6 = 5.
Step 2: Check remainder_index[5] = 0 (seen before).
Step 3: Distance = 2 - 0 = 2 ≥ 2, so condition satisfied.
Step 4: Return True, indicating a subarray from index 1 to 2 has sum divisible by 6.

testCases.expected: Update remainder_index[2] to 1 (overwrite not allowed, but here it's same remainder, so check distance first)
Check remainder_index[0] = -1, but since remainder is 0 and index - (-1) = 1 < 2, continue

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prefix Sum Remainders and Modular Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand why same remainders in prefix sums indicate divisible subarrays.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is based on modular arithmetic: if you have two prefix sums, prefix_sum[i] and prefix_sum[j] with i < j, and they leave the same remainder when divided by k, then the subarray from i+1 to j has a sum that is a multiple of k. Mathematically, let prefix_sum[i] = a*k + r and prefix_sum[j] = b*k + r, where r is the common remainder. Then, prefix_sum[j] - prefix_sum[i] = (b*k + r) - (a*k + r) = (b-a)*k, which is divisible by k. This means the sum of elements from index i+1 to j is divisible by k. We use this property to avoid checking all subarrays explicitly. By computing prefix sums modulo k as we traverse the array, we can store the first occurrence of each remainder. When we encounter the same remainder again, we check if the distance between indices is at least 2 (to ensure subarray length ≥ 2). This reduces the problem from O(n²) to O(n). Time complexity for this logic is O(n) for computing remainders, and space is O(k) for storing remainders. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the key sub-routine. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the modulo operation with negative numbers, which requires adjustment to positive remainders. Real code pattern: running_sum = 0; for each num in nums: running_sum += num; remainder = running_sum % k; if remainder < 0: remainder += k;.

examples.output: Remainders: [5, 1, 5], same remainder 5 at indices 0 and 2 indicates subarray [2,4] sum divisible by 6
examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute prefix sums: 23, 23+2=25, 25+4=29.
Step 2: Compute remainders modulo 6: 23%6=5, 25%6=1, 29%6=5.
Step 3: Remainder 5 occurs at index 0 (prefix sum 23) and index 2 (prefix sum 29).
Step 4: Difference in prefix sums: 29-23=6, which is divisible by 6.
Step 5: Corresponding subarray: from index 1 to 2 in nums: [2,4], sum=6.

examples.output: Remainders: [2, 2, 2], same remainder 2 at indices 0,1,2 indicates multiple subarrays divisible by 3
examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute prefix sums: 5, 5+0=5, 5+0+0=5.
Step 2: Compute remainders modulo 3: 5%3=2, 5%3=2, 5%3=2.
Step 3: Remainder 2 occurs at indices 0,1,2.
Step 4: For i=0, j=1: prefix_sum[1]-prefix_sum[0]=0, divisible by 3, subarray [0].
Step 5: For i=0, j=2: prefix_sum[2]-prefix_sum[0]=0, divisible by 3, subarray [0,0].
Step 6: Check distance condition: e.g., j-i >= 2 for subarray length ≥ 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Running Sum and Modulo with Negative Adjustment
ladders.desc: Calculate prefix sums and handle modulo for negative numbers.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves computing the running sum (prefix sum) of an array and then finding its remainder when divided by k, with special handling for negative numbers. The running sum is updated by adding each element as we traverse the array. To compute the remainder, we use the modulo operator (%). However, in many programming languages, the modulo operation can return negative results for negative dividends. For example, -1 % 6 might give -1 instead of 5. Since we need remainders in the range [0, k-1] to use as indices in an array, we must adjust negative remainders by adding k. This ensures consistency. The intuition is that in modular arithmetic, -1 mod 6 is equivalent to 5 mod 6 because -1 + 6 = 5. This adjustment is crucial to avoid index errors and incorrect logic. Time complexity is O(1) per element for these operations. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. Connection to L2: This is used to compute the remainders in the core logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to adjust negative remainders, which can lead to out-of-bounds array access or missed matches. Real code pattern: running_sum = 0; for num in nums: running_sum += num; remainder = running_sum % k; if remainder < 0: remainder += k;.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize running_sum = 0.
Step 2: Add 23: running_sum = 23. Remainder = 23 % 6 = 5 (positive).
Step 3: Add 2: running_sum = 25. Remainder = 25 % 6 = 1 (positive).
Step 4: Add 4: running_sum = 29. Remainder = 29 % 6 = 5 (positive).

Step 1: Initialize running_sum = 0.
Step 2: Add -1: running_sum = -1. Remainder = -1 % 6 = -1 (negative). Adjust: -1 + 6 = 5.
Step 3: Add -2: running_sum = -3. Remainder = -3 % 6 = -3. Adjust: -3 + 6 = 3.
Step 4: Add -3: running_sum = -6. Remainder = -6 % 6 = 0 (no adjustment needed).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Arithmetic and Array Indexing
ladders.desc: Perform modulo operations and use results to index into an array.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two key skills: computing the modulo of an integer and using that result as an index to access or update an array. Modulo (often denoted as % in programming) gives the remainder when one number is divided by another. For example, 23 % 6 = 5 because 23 divided by 6 is 3 with remainder 5. This operation is fundamental in many algorithms for hashing or cyclic patterns. Once we have a remainder r, which lies between 0 and k-1 (after adjustment for negatives), we can use it to index into an array of size k. For instance, if k=6, remainders range from 0 to 5, so we can create an array 'remainder_index' of length 6. We then use remainder as an index to store or retrieve values, such as storing the first index where that remainder occurred. This allows O(1) access and update. Time complexity is O(1) for each modulo and array operation. Space complexity is O(k) for the array. Connection to L3: This operation is used to compute remainders and manage the remainder_index array. Common mistakes include using modulo without understanding its behavior with negative numbers, or accessing array indices out of bounds if remainder is not in [0, k-1]. Real code pattern: remainder = number % k; if remainder < 0: remainder += k; value = array[remainder]; array[remainder] = new_value.

examples.input: Compute 29 % 6 and use result to index an array of size 6
examples.trace:
Step 1: Calculate 29 % 6: 29 divided by 6 is 4 with remainder 5, so remainder = 5.
Step 2: Check if remainder is in range 0 to 5: yes.
Step 3: Use remainder as index: array[5] can be read or updated.
Step 4: For example, if array = [-1, -1, -1, -1, -1, -1], array[5] gives -1.

examples.input: Compute -3 % 6, adjust if negative, then index array
examples.trace:
Step 1: Calculate -3 % 6: in many languages, this gives -3.
Step 2: Since remainder is negative, adjust: -3 + 6 = 3.
Step 3: Now remainder = 3, which is in range 0 to 5.
Step 4: Use as index: array[3] can be accessed.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Arithmetic Operations
ladders.desc: Understand arrays, indexing, and simple math like addition and modulo.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the most basic concepts needed: arrays, indexing, and arithmetic operations like addition and modulo. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each accessible by an index starting from 0. For example, an array 'arr' of size k has indices 0, 1, ..., k-1, and you can access an element with arr[i]. Indexing must be within bounds to avoid errors. Addition is a basic arithmetic operation where you sum numbers, e.g., 5 + 3 = 8. Modulo is another operation that finds the remainder when dividing one number by another. For instance, 10 % 3 = 1 because 10 divided by 3 is 3 with remainder 1. These operations are building blocks for more complex algorithms. In the context of our problem, we use arrays to store information about remainders, addition to compute running sums, and modulo to find remainders. Time and space are not analyzed here as these are constant-time operations. Connection to L4: These operations are combined to perform modulo arithmetic and array indexing. Common mistakes include confusing array indices (e.g., starting from 1 instead of 0) or misunderstanding modulo results. Real code pattern: arr = [0] * k;  # create array of size k with zeros; sum = a + b; remainder = a % b.

examples.input: Create an array of size 3, set arr[0] = 5, arr[1] = 10, then compute (arr[0] + arr[1]) % 3
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create array arr of size 3: initially [0, 0, 0].
Step 2: Set arr[0] = 5: arr becomes [5, 0, 0].
Step 3: Set arr[1] = 10: arr becomes [5, 10, 0].
Step 4: Compute arr[0] + arr[1] = 5 + 10 = 15.
Step 5: Compute 15 % 3: 15 divided by 3 is 5 with remainder 0, so result = 0.

examples.input: Given numbers 7 and 4, compute 7 % 4 and use result as index in array of size 4
examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute 7 % 4: 7 divided by 4 is 1 with remainder 3, so remainder = 3.
Step 2: Check if remainder is within array indices 0 to 3: yes, 3 is valid.
Step 3: In an array arr of size 4, e.g., arr = [a, b, c, d], index 3 refers to element d.
Step 4: You can read arr[3] or set arr[3] to a value.

testCases.input: Array size 5, set arr[2] = 8, compute arr[2] + 3
Array size 2, arr = [1, 4], compute arr[0] % arr[1]
Array size 3, set all elements to 0, then set arr[1] = 5
Array size 4, arr = [2, 3, 5, 7], compute arr[0] + arr[2]
Array size 1, set arr[0] = 10, compute arr[0] % 2
